name: ELIZA
parameter_count: Not applicable — rule-based pattern matching with approximately 200 rules
training_approach: Hand-written pattern matching rules and scripts; no machine learning
key_innovations: First program to simulate human-like conversation (first chatbot)
DOCTOR script simulated a Rogerian psychotherapist using simple pattern matching and reflection
Demonstrated the ELIZA effect — humans' tendency to attribute understanding to machines that produce seemingly intelligent responses
Showed that simple rules could create surprisingly convincing conversational behavior

benchmark_highlights.note: Predates formal AI benchmarks; evaluated by human perception of conversational quality

lineage_path: Rule-based systems -> ELIZA -> PARRY -> A.L.I.C.E. -> Modern chatbots
cultural_impact: ELIZA, created by Joseph Weizenbaum at MIT, was the first program many people ever 'talked' to. The ELIZA effect — where users attributed genuine understanding to the program — deeply troubled Weizenbaum himself and led him to become one of AI's earliest critics. ELIZA is the direct ancestor of every chatbot and conversational AI system that followed.
